parts.prompt: Based on VSEPR theory, predict the geometry around the atom in compound Y.

required_elements: For the correct answer: Point 01 Tetrahedral

parts.prompt:
A student claims that compound Y has a higher boiling point than that of compound X
because compound Y has stronger London dispersion forces. Do you agree or disagree?
Justify your answer.
82°C

scoring_points.description: For the correct answer and a valid justification

required_elements: For the correct answer and a valid justification: Point 02 Agree. Compound Y has a larger, more polarizable electron cloud because the Si atom has more occupied electron shells than the C atom, giving compound Y stronger London dispersion forces and a higher boiling point than compound X.
alternatives.correct_example: Agree. Compound Y has a larger, more polarizable electron cloud because the Si atom has more occupied electron shells than the C atom, giving compound Y stronger London dispersion forces and a higher boiling point than compound X.

parts.prompt:
An equimolar mixture of the two compounds is heated. When the mixture reaches ,
which compound will have the higher vapor pressure? Justify your answer.
198°C

scoring_points.description: For the correct answer and a valid justification. Point 03

required_elements: For the correct answer and a valid justification. Point 03
alternatives.correct_example: Examples of acceptable responses may include the following: Y, so molecules of X are more likely to be in the gas phase at 82°C and would

parts.prompt:
The mixture is heated to in a sealed, rigid 12.5 L container, at which point both
substances are gases and the total pressure in the container is 2.30 atm. Calculate the number
of moles of gas particles in the container.

required_elements: For the correct calculated value: Point 04 PV = nRT PV (2.30 atm)(12.5 L) n = = = 0.744 mol RT (0.08206 L⋅atm)(471K) K⋅mol
alternatives.correct_example: PV = nRT PV (2.30 atm)(12.5 L) n = = = 0.744 mol RT (0.08206 L⋅atm)(471K) K⋅mol
